---
title: "UIAO Modernization Master Plan — Boundary Modernization Appendix"
subtitle: "Authorization-boundary redesign and the Signal Restoration Framework"
doc-type: master-plan
phase: "Appendix to Phase 1 — Boundary Modernization"
audience: "CIO · CISO · ISSO · Authorizing Official (AO) · 3PAO · Network and Security Architects"
derived-from: "phase-1-architecture.qmd"
upstream:
  - "phase-0-planning.qmd §4 FedRAMP Boundary Gap"
  - "phase-1-architecture.qmd §3 Inter-Plane Interfaces"
  - "phase-1-architecture.qmd §4 Signal Taxonomy"
  - "phase-1-architecture.qmd §7 Conformance Criteria"
date: 2026-04-26
---

# UIAO Modernization Master Plan — Boundary Modernization Appendix {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
## Series Map — you are reading **document 3 of 5**
[Series index](index.qmd) ·
[Phase 0 Planning](phase-0-planning.qmd) →
[Phase 1 Architecture](phase-1-architecture.qmd) →
**Boundary Modernization Appendix** (this document) →
[Compliance & ATO Alignment](compliance-and-ato-alignment.qmd) →
[90-Day Execution Plan](90-day-execution-plan.qmd)
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Document Purpose
This appendix expands the Boundary Gap framing from
[Phase 0 §4](phase-0-planning.qmd#fedramp-boundary-gap-architectural-dependency)
into a concrete redesign of the authorization boundary. It defines the
boundary class taxonomy, the per-class signal-continuity requirements,
the normative Signal Restoration Framework, the sequencing constraints
the boundary redesign imposes on downstream planes, and the conformance
criteria a boundary redesign must satisfy. It resolves
[Phase 1 §8 Open Question #2](phase-1-architecture.qmd#open-architecture-questions).
:::

---

## 1. Scope and Position in the Program

The authorization boundary is the surface across which signals,
identities, and traffic transition between domains of differing trust,
control, or compliance authority. In a FedRAMP Rev 5 system, the
boundary is defined by the System Security Plan; in operational reality,
the boundary is crossed many times per user action, by many distinct
mechanisms, each of which can attenuate signal.

This appendix treats the boundary as an *architectural object* with its
own redesign plan, not a documentation artifact. Every boundary crossing
must produce a `boundary-crossing` provenance record (Phase 1 §3.2) and
must demonstrably preserve the six primary signal classes (Phase 1
§4.1).

This appendix does not select vendors, prescribe transports, or commit
to a deployment sequence. Those decisions belong to adapter-level design
and to the 90-Day Execution Plan respectively.

---

## 2. Boundary Class Taxonomy

UIAO recognizes six boundary classes. Each has distinct continuity
requirements and distinct failure modes when continuity is not preserved.

| # | Boundary Class | Crossing Example | Primary Failure Mode When Attenuated |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identity Boundary** | On-prem directory ↔ cloud identity provider | Identity flattening; loss of OU-encoded delegation |
| 2 | **Network Boundary** | Field-office WAN ↔ central egress ↔ cloud SaaS | Path-dependent policy divergence; regional drift |
| 3 | **Tenant Boundary** | Cloud-tenant ↔ cloud-tenant (commercial ↔ GCC) | Cross-tenant signal loss; orphaned principals |
| 4 | **Plane Boundary** | Substrate plane ↔ Enforcement plane | Substrate state consumed without arbitration |
| 5 | **Telemetry Boundary** | Per-system log surface ↔ unified evidence graph | Per-journey reconstruction becomes infeasible |
| 6 | **Authorization Boundary** | In-scope FedRAMP system ↔ adjacent / external system | Evidence chain resets at the boundary; cATO interrupted |

Boundary classes are not mutually exclusive: a single user action may
cross all six in sequence. The Signal Restoration Framework (§4)
specifies how each class preserves the six primary signal classes.

---

## 3. Continuity Requirements per Boundary Class

Each boundary class is required to preserve the six primary signal
classes from Phase 1 §4.1 (Identity, Location, Session, Telemetry,
Media, Policy). Where preservation is not technically feasible at a
given crossing, the appendix requires *attributed degradation*: a
provenance record that names the lost signal class and the compensating
control.

### 3.1 Continuity Matrix

The following matrix is the normative continuity requirement. Cells
marked **R** are required end-to-end; cells marked **A** require
attributed degradation if not preserved; cells marked **N/A** mean the
signal class does not apply at that boundary.

| Boundary ↓ / Signal → | Identity | Location | Session | Telemetry | Media | Policy |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Identity Boundary** | R | A | R | R | N/A | R |
| **Network Boundary** | R | R | R | R | R | R |
| **Tenant Boundary** | R | A | R | R | A | R |
| **Plane Boundary** | R | A | R | R | N/A | R |
| **Telemetry Boundary** | R | R | R | R | R | R |
| **Authorization Boundary** | R | R | R | R | R | R |

### 3.2 Compensating Controls for Attributed Degradation

Where the matrix permits attributed degradation, the appendix mandates:

1. A `boundary-crossing` provenance record naming the lost signal class.
2. A reference to the compensating control in the Compliance plane.
3. A re-acquisition mechanism downstream (e.g. re-authentication,
   re-attestation, re-classification of media).
4. A drift threshold that escalates when attributed degradation exceeds
   the negotiated rate.

Attributed degradation is not a relaxation of the conformance criteria;
it is a governed exception with named owner, named compensating control,
and a measurable threshold.

---

## 4. Signal Restoration Framework

The Signal Restoration Framework (SRF) is the normative pattern by
which the six primary signal classes are preserved across every boundary
crossing. It is composed of five layers, each addressing a distinct
mechanism of signal loss.

### 4.1 Framework Layers

| Layer | Purpose | What It Restores |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Carry** | Ensure the signal traverses the boundary in an attributed envelope | Identity, Session, Policy |
| **L2 — Reconstruct** | Rebuild signal context lost in transit using canonical sources | Identity, Location |
| **L3 — Re-attest** | Re-establish posture or authentication where carry is impossible | Session, Identity |
| **L4 — Capture** | Emit a provenance record at every crossing, regardless of preservation | Telemetry, Provenance |
| **L5 — Arbitrate** | Resolve conflicting signals from multiple sources via canonical authority | All classes |

A conforming boundary redesign applies the smallest set of layers
sufficient to satisfy §3.1 for that boundary class. L4 is mandatory for
every crossing without exception.

### 4.2 Framework Invariants

Three invariants govern the framework:

1. **Capture is unconditional.** L4 fires on every crossing, including
   crossings that preserve all signals; the absence of a crossing record
   is itself drift.
2. **Reconstruction must cite canonical source.** L2 may not synthesize
   signal from heuristics; it must cite a canonical source whose
   provenance is itself reconstructable.
3. **Arbitration is the Compliance plane's right.** When L5 must choose
   among conflicting signals, the canonical-authority decision is
   recorded as a Compliance-plane assertion, not a substrate-plane
   override.

### 4.3 What the Framework Is Not

- It is not a transport — it does not specify message buses, APIs, or
  protocols.
- It is not a vendor selection — it does not commit to a CASB, IdP, SASE
  fabric, or telemetry pipeline.
- It is not a one-time migration — it is a continuous operating posture
  evidenced through Continuous ATO.

---

## 5. Sequencing Constraints on Downstream Planes

Because every downstream plane depends on signals the Boundary Gap
attenuates, the boundary redesign imposes the following sequencing
constraints. The 90-Day Execution Plan must respect them.

| # | Constraint | Affected Plane(s) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | No Zero Trust enforcement is treated as conformant until the Identity and Network boundaries satisfy §3.1 row R cells | Security |
| 2 | No SCuBA conformance assertion is treated as evidenced until the Tenant and Telemetry boundaries satisfy §3.1 | Compliance, Drift Detection |
| 3 | No cATO authorization-state may derive from evidence whose chain breaks at an unmodernized boundary | Continuous ATO |
| 4 | No POA&M closure may rely on compensating controls whose attributed-degradation rate exceeds the negotiated threshold | Compliance |
| 5 | No drift-record may be classified without an upstream `boundary-crossing` record from the implicated crossing | Drift Detection |

These constraints are architectural, not procedural. They are encoded
into the conformance check for each downstream plane.

---

## 6. Conformance Criteria for Boundary Modernization

A boundary redesign is conforming when, and only when, all of the
following are demonstrably true:

| # | Criterion | Evidence Surface |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | All six boundary classes are inventoried with named owners | Boundary registry |
| 2 | Every crossing emits a `boundary-crossing` provenance record (SRF L4) | Provenance graph completeness scan over a defined window |
| 3 | The §3.1 continuity matrix is satisfied for every crossing instance | Per-crossing signal preservation report |
| 4 | Attributed degradation, where used, has a named compensating control and a measurable threshold | Compensating-control registry |
| 5 | Reconstruction (SRF L2) cites canonical sources only, with reconstructable provenance | L2 reconstruction audit |
| 6 | Arbitration (SRF L5) decisions are recorded as Compliance-plane assertions | Arbitration ledger |
| 7 | Sequencing constraints from §5 are enforced as conformance gates on downstream planes | Cross-plane conformance check |
| 8 | Boundary modernization posture is sustained, not point-in-time | Continuous-ATO authorization-state derivation trace |

A deployment that satisfies criteria 1–4 is *boundary-evidenced*; one
that additionally satisfies 5–8 is *boundary-conforming*. Phase 0 §7
treats the gap between these two states as a foundational risk; this
appendix makes the gap measurable.

---

## 7. Risks Specific to Boundary Modernization

The Phase 0 §7 risk register names six program-level risks. The
following five are specific to the boundary redesign and are tracked
inside this appendix.

| # | Risk | Mitigation Posture |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | Boundary inventory is incomplete; unknown crossings exist | Discovery scan as a gating activity in the 90-Day Plan |
| B2 | Attributed-degradation rate creeps upward as exceptions accumulate | Threshold and trend tracked by Drift Detection; escalation to AO |
| B3 | Reconstruction (SRF L2) drifts toward heuristic synthesis | L2 reconstruction audit per §6 criterion 5 |
| B4 | Arbitration (SRF L5) is exercised by substrate planes outside Compliance | Arbitration ledger reviewed at every cATO cycle |
| B5 | Sequencing constraints are bypassed under schedule pressure | Constraints enforced as automated conformance gates, not policy |

Each risk has an explicit owner in the Boundary Modernization plane and
an escalation path through the Compliance plane to the AO.

---

## 8. Hand-off to Downstream Phases

This appendix closes when ratified. Downstream consumption is as
follows:

- **Compliance & ATO Alignment** consumes §3 (Continuity Matrix), §4
  (SRF Layers and Invariants), and §6 (Conformance Criteria) to bind
  controls to boundary-evidenced and boundary-conforming states, and to
  define the cATO authorization-state derivation rule.
- **90-Day Execution Plan** consumes §2 (Boundary Class Taxonomy), §5
  (Sequencing Constraints), and §7 (Risks) to schedule discovery,
  capture, and remediation activities in a sequence that respects the
  architectural prerequisites.

No downstream commitment is made against an unratified Boundary
Modernization Appendix; this is the same sequencing rule established in
Phase 0 §8 and restated in Phase 1 §9.

---

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
## Document Governance
This Boundary Modernization Appendix is a canonical program artifact.
Changes are governed by the canon-change protocol; substantive revisions
require an ADR, a schema check, and a drift scan. The appendix derives
from the Phase 1 Architecture Document and is the upstream source for
the Compliance & ATO Alignment document and the 90-Day Execution Plan.
:::
